title: Transport Detection of Whirlpools in GaAs Electron Liquid
authors: Dmitry A. Egorov, Dmitriy A. Pokhabov, Evgeny Yu. Zhdanov, Andrey A. Shevyrin, Askhat K. Bakarov, Arthur G. Pogosov
abstract: We report the formation of large-scale steady-state whirlpools in a GaAs-based two-dimensional electron liquid and demonstrate them by straightforward transport measurements. A whirlpool forming inside a circular cavity adjoining a wide conducting channel appears as a negative four-terminal resistance over a broad range of temperatures and cavity sizes. The effect scales with the Gurzhi length, in quantitative accord with the hydrodynamic analogy. Obtained results firmly establish this analogy and probe the limits of its applicability.
summary: This paper reports the experimental detection of hydrodynamic whirlpools in a GaAs electron liquid using simple transport measurements. By observing negative resistance that scales with the Gurzhi length, the authors provide strong evidence for electronic vorticity and challenge existing theoretical relations for electron viscosity.
main_problem: The direct experimental observation and characterization of large-scale steady-state hydrodynamic electron vortices (whirlpools) in a 2D electron liquid have been difficult due to instrumentation and material constraints.
main_result: The authors demonstrate the formation of whirlpools in a GaAs-based 2D electron liquid via negative four-terminal resistance and confirm that the transition scales universally with the Gurzhi length, suggesting a breakdown of the standard viscosity-scattering relation.
method: The study uses purely transport-based measurements of four-terminal resistance and potential difference, complemented by numerical hydrodynamic simulations and scaling analysis.
model_or_system: A high-mobility two-dimensional electron gas (2DEG) in a GaAs/AlGaAs heterostructure, featuring circular cavities adjoining a wide conducting channel.
key_observables: Negative four-terminal resistance, potential difference, and current streamlines.
important_parameters: Gurzhi length (D_nu), electron viscosity (nu), momentum relaxation length (l_mr), electron-electron scattering length (l_ee), and cavity diameter (D).
main_assumptions: The simulations assume a 'slip boundary condition' limit, consistent with perfect slip electron flow in GaAs.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the device geometry and simulated current/potential maps; Fig 2 shows V vs I curves and temperature-dependent resistance; Fig 3 shows the scaling of the Gurzhi length at the transition temperature; Fig 4 shows resistance in a reference geometry with an etched strip to confirm the absence of vortices.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of vortex detection, describes the GaAs/AlGaAs experimental platform and geometry, presents transport measurements and scaling analysis, compares results to numerical simulations, and discusses the implications for electron viscosity theory.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
